text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 21 September 2025 

Dr Aisha Patel  
St. George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Aisha Patel,  

Eleanor Wright, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9012738846  
Flat 12, 28 Arundel House, Streatham, London SW16 2JP 

Diagnoses:  

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness, likely temporal lobe onset (diagnosed 2023)  
MRI brain (2023): subtle left mesial temporal sclerosis suspected  
EEG (2023): left temporal sharp waves

Current Medication:  

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg bd  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (intermittent)  
Rescue: Midazolam buccal 10 mg prn prolonged episode

Plan:  

- Optimise antiseizure therapy: increase Levetiracetam to 1,500 mg bd if tolerated; monitor mood/irritability  
- Consider add-on Lacosamide if inadequate response after 6 weeks  
- Sleep hygiene and alcohol moderation reinforced; avoid missed doses  
- Safety advice regarding bathing at night; bedroom adaptations discussed  
- DVLA guidance reiterated; patient already not driving  
- Arrange ambulatory EEG and repeat MRI with epilepsy protocol  
- Follow up in 8–10 weeks; earlier review if events escalate

I reviewed Eleanor Wright in my clinic today. They described a 2-year history of focal impaired awareness episodes characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, brief behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and confusion lasting 1–2 minutes, with post-event fatigue. Over the last 3 months there has been a clear nocturnal clustering pattern with seizures every night, typically between 02:00 and 04:00, witnessed by their partner who noted automatisms and brief vocalisation. There have been no generalised tonic-clonic seizures. Triggers include sleep deprivation and stress; they deny excess alcohol. Adherence has been good, though they report occasional missed evening doses prior to worsening. Mood is stable on current regimen. 

Examination today was unremarkable neurologically. Prior EEG demonstrated left temporal sharp wave discharges and MRI suggested left mesial temporal sclerosis, consistent with focal epilepsy. Given the persistent nocturnal events—described as seizures every night—despite current dosing, we discussed dose optimisation and potential add-on therapy. We also reviewed safety at night (e.g. low bed height, avoiding locked bathroom doors). 

I will arrange an ambulatory EEG to sample sleep and a repeat MRI with an epilepsy protocol to clarify the structural substrate. We discussed the possibility of referral to the surgical pathway if seizures remain refractory despite two adequately tried medications. Consent for data sharing within the epilepsy MDT was obtained. 

Please note some received external correspondence contained minor text artefacts (Ã‚Â·, Ã©, ï¿½, â€) that do not affect clinical interpretation. ï¿½ 

With best wishes,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "seizures every night" and "clear nocturnal clustering pattern with seizures every night, typically between 02:00 and 04:00." This indicates daily occurrence during the last 3 months. No other frequency is given that is higher than daily. Therefore, normalize to 1 per day.
